question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted Base32-encoded alias that resolves to an entity with `deleted = NULL` (not `deleted = true`) via the `deleted is not true` predicate, causing the GraphQL API to return a logically-deleted account as active, leading downstream consumers to treat it as a valid unfrozen account and route funds into it, permanently locking those funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user supply an EVM address that matches an entity whose `deleted` column is NULL (not explicitly `false`), causing `findByEvmAddress` to return it due to `deleted is not true` semantics, and have a dependent system treat that entity as a live unfrozen account, resulting in funds being sent to an account that is actually in a frozen or indeterminate state?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the behavioral difference between `deleted is not true` (used in graphql EntityRepository) and `deleted <> true` (used in importer/rest-java EntityRepository) to resolve an alias to a different entity than other subsystems resolve it to, causing a freeze-state mismatch where the GraphQL layer believes an account is active while the network has it frozen, leading to permanent fund loss?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user craft a 20-byte EVM address whose first 12 bytes are all zero, causing `EntityServiceImpl.getByEvmAddressAndType` to fall back to the inherited `CrudRepository.findById` (which has no `deleted` filter at all), returning a frozen or deleted entity as if it were active, and causing funds routed to that address to be permanently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user provide a malformed or oversized Base32 alias string that, after `decodeBase32` decoding in `EntityServiceImpl.getByAliasAndType`, produces a byte array that collides with the alias of a frozen account, causing `findByAlias` to return that frozen account and leading to funds being directed to a permanently frozen destination?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a hex-encoded EVM address with a `0x` prefix that, after `decodeEvmAddress` strips the prefix and decodes it, matches the `evm_address` of an entity whose token associations are all in `FROZEN` state, causing the GraphQL API to return that entity as a valid active account and leading callers to send tokens to it where they become permanently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user observe that `findByAlias` returns the full `Entity` object (including `key`, `balance`, `type`) without any freeze-status field, and exploit the absence of freeze-status information in the returned entity to convince an integrating application that an account is safe to receive tokens, when in fact its token associations are frozen, causing permanent fund loss?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `findByEvmAddress` returns `select * from entity` (all columns) but the `entity` table contains no `freeze_status` column, causing GraphQL consumers to incorrectly infer that an account is not frozen based on the absence of a freeze field, and route funds to a frozen account where they become permanently locked?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a valid alias for an account that has `deleted = NULL` (set during an in-progress deletion transaction not yet finalized in the mirror node), causing `findByAlias` to return it as active due to `deleted is not true`, and have funds sent to it during the window before deletion is confirmed, resulting in those funds being permanently frozen in an account that is being deleted?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a replication lag in the mirror node database where a `TokenFreeze` transaction has been applied on-chain but not yet reflected in the `entity` table, causing `findByEvmAddress` to return an entity that appears active and unfrozen, leading to funds being sent to a now-frozen account and becoming permanently locked?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user provide an alias byte array of length zero (empty), causing `findByAlias` to execute `select * from entity where alias = '' and deleted is not true`, potentially matching an entity with an empty alias field, and if that entity has frozen token associations, cause funds directed to it to be permanently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user supply an EVM address of incorrect length (e.g., fewer than 20 bytes), causing `findByEvmAddress` to execute a native SQL query with a truncated byte array that matches an unintended entity record, and if that entity is associated with frozen tokens, cause funds routed to it to be permanently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `findByAlias` uses a native SQL query (`nativeQuery = true`) with positional parameter `?1`, and that no input length or content validation is performed before the query, to pass a byte array that matches the alias of a contract account (not an ACCOUNT type), and since type filtering happens after the query in `EntityServiceImpl`, observe timing or error-based side-channel information about frozen contract states?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user craft a GraphQL query that calls the `account` resolver with an `evmAddress` input that resolves via `findByEvmAddress` to an entity whose `expirationTimestamp` has passed, causing the account to be in an auto-renew grace period where token transfers are blocked (effectively frozen), and have the GraphQL API return it as a valid active account, leading to permanent fund loss?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a uniqueness guarantee in `findByAlias` — since the query returns `Optional<Entity>` but does not enforce that only one row matches — to trigger a non-deterministic result when two entities share the same alias (one frozen, one not), causing the frozen entity to be returned and funds directed to it to be permanently locked?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a uniqueness guarantee in `findByEvmAddress` — since the native SQL query does not include `LIMIT 1` — to trigger undefined behavior when multiple entities share the same `evm_address`, potentially returning a frozen entity instead of the intended active one, causing funds to be permanently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a GraphQL `account` query with an alias that resolves to an entity of type `CONTRACT` (not `ACCOUNT`), and since `EntityServiceImpl.getByAliasAndType` filters by type after calling `findByAlias`, observe whether the entity exists (via response timing or error differences), and use this to enumerate frozen contract accounts that hold funds, enabling targeted freeze-based attacks?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `ByteBuffer` parsing logic in `EntityServiceImpl.getByEvmAddressAndType` — where a 20-byte address with first 4 bytes as zero int and next 8 bytes as zero long triggers `findById` instead of `findByEvmAddress` — to bypass the `deleted is not true` filter entirely, causing a deleted-but-frozen entity to be returned as active and funds sent to it to be permanently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user provide a Base32 alias that decodes to a byte array matching the `alias` column of an entity that has `receiverSigRequired = true` and frozen token associations, causing `findByAlias` to return it as a valid account, and have an integrating system attempt to send tokens to it without the required signature, resulting in the transaction failing and funds being locked in an intermediate state?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `decodeEvmAddress` in `GraphQlUtils` returns an empty byte array (`ArrayUtils.EMPTY_BYTE_ARRAY`) when the input is null, and that this empty array is then passed to `findByEvmAddress`, potentially matching an entity with a null or empty `evm_address` column, and if that entity has frozen token associations, cause funds to be permanently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the mirror node's read-only nature — where `findByAlias` reflects a potentially stale snapshot of the `entity` table — to query an account during the window between a `TokenUnfreeze` transaction being submitted on-chain and it being ingested by the mirror node, causing the API to report the account as still frozen and blocking legitimate fund transfers that should succeed?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `findByEvmAddress` returns the full `Entity` object including the `key` field (raw protobuf bytes), and that the absence of a `freezeKey` in the returned entity data does not mean the account cannot be frozen (freeze key is on the token, not the account), to mislead integrating applications into believing an account is immune to freezing, causing funds to be sent to an account that is subsequently frozen?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a GraphQL query with an alias that resolves to an entity with `permanentRemoval = true`, and since `findByAlias` only checks `deleted is not true` without checking `permanentRemoval`, have the entity returned as active, causing funds directed to it to be permanently frozen in an account scheduled for permanent removal?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `findByEvmAddress` does not check `permanentRemoval` or `expirationTimestamp`, causing it to return an expired entity as active, and have funds sent to that expired account become permanently frozen because the account can no longer receive or send tokens after expiration?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@GraphQlRepository` annotation on `EntityRepository` — which exposes the repository directly to the GraphQL data fetching layer — to bypass the type-filtering logic in `EntityServiceImpl.getByAliasAndType` by directly invoking the repository method through a crafted GraphQL introspection or data-fetching path, returning a frozen entity of any type and causing funds to be directed to it?
